cards.front: What type of routing protocol is OSPF?
cards.back: Link-state, classless, IGP. Uses Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to compute the best path. Open standard (RFC 2328 for OSPFv2 IPv4, RFC 5340 for OSPFv3 IPv6). AD = 110.

cards.front: List the 7 OSPF neighbor states in order.
cards.back: Down → Init → 2-Way → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full. Adjacency must reach FULL for routes to be exchanged. (DR/BDR adjacencies progress to Full; non-DR/BDR pairs stop at 2-Way on multi-access networks.)

cards.front: What 5 conditions must match for OSPF neighbors to form an adjacency?
cards.back: (1) Same area ID. (2) Same hello/dead interval. (3) Same subnet (must be on same network/mask). (4) Same authentication (if enabled). (5) Matching MTU (must be the same). (6) Compatible network type. (7) Both must agree on stub flags.

cards.front: Default hello and dead timers on OSPF for broadcast/PtP networks?
cards.back: Hello = 10 seconds, Dead = 40 seconds (4× hello). On NBMA / non-broadcast networks: Hello = 30, Dead = 120. Both ends must match exactly or no adjacency.

cards.front: What is an OSPF DR/BDR and why does OSPF elect them?
cards.back: On multi-access networks (broadcast Ethernet), to avoid every router becoming neighbors with every other router (n² adjacencies), one Designated Router and one Backup are elected. All other routers (DROther) form full adjacency only with DR and BDR. DR floods LSAs on behalf of the segment.

cards.front: How is the OSPF DR elected, and what are the tiebreakers?
cards.back: (1) Highest OSPF priority (default 1, range 0-255; priority 0 = ineligible). (2) Highest router-ID. NOT preemptive — once elected, the DR stays until it dies, even if a higher-priority router joins later. To force re-election: clear OSPF process or shut/no-shut.

cards.front: How is the OSPF Router-ID determined?
cards.back: Order of preference: (1) Manually configured `router-id A.B.C.D`. (2) Highest IP on a loopback interface. (3) Highest IP on any active interface. Best practice: explicitly set router-id with a loopback so it's stable.

cards.front: What is a Link State Advertisement (LSA), and what's the LSDB?
cards.back: LSA = an OSPF message describing one piece of network state (a router, a link, an external route, etc.). The LSDB (Link-State Database) is the complete set of LSAs each router has — identical across all routers in the same area. SPF runs against the LSDB.

cards.front: What is the purpose of OSPF AREAS?
cards.back: Limit LSA flooding scope, reduce LSDB size, and contain SPF recalculations. Each area has its own LSDB. Area 0 (backbone) must connect to all other areas. Routers connecting two areas are ABRs (Area Border Routers).

cards.front: Classify dynamic routing protocols: IGP vs EGP, and Distance-Vector / Link-State / Path-Vector — name examples.
cards.back: IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol — within one AS): Distance-Vector — RIP, EIGRP. Link-State — OSPF, IS-IS. EGP (between ASes): Path-Vector — BGP. (EIGRP is sometimes called 'advanced distance-vector' / hybrid.) Distance-vector = 'routing by rumor', sends route + metric to neighbors. Link-state = floods topology info, every router builds full map (more CPU but faster convergence).

cards.front: Memorize Cisco default Administrative Distances for: Connected, Static, eBGP, EIGRP, IGRP, OSPF, IS-IS, RIP, EIGRP-external, iBGP, Unknown.
cards.back: Connected = 0. Static = 1. eBGP = 20. EIGRP (internal) = 90. IGRP = 100. OSPF = 110. IS-IS = 115. RIP = 120. EIGRP (external) = 170. iBGP = 200. Unusable/Unknown = 255 (route is not installed). Lower AD wins between protocols.

cards.front: What is ECMP, and which CCNA routing protocols support equal vs unequal cost load balancing?
cards.back: ECMP (Equal-Cost Multi-Path) load balances across multiple routes with identical metric. RIP, OSPF, EIGRP, BGP all do ECMP (default 4 paths, configurable with `maximum-paths`). Unequal-cost: only EIGRP supports it (via the `variance` multiplier — only over feasible-successor routes). RIP and OSPF do NOT do unequal-cost.

cards.front: Which IPv4 multicast addresses do RIPv2, EIGRP, OSPF, and OSPF DR/BDR use?
cards.back: RIPv2 → 224.0.0.9 (RIPv1 = broadcast). EIGRP → 224.0.0.10. OSPF (all OSPF routers) → 224.0.0.5. OSPF DR/BDR (all DRouters) → 224.0.0.6. All four are link-local multicast (TTL=1, never forwarded by routers).

cards.front: RIP key facts: max hop count, message types, version transport, configuration mode?
cards.back: Metric = hop count, max 15 (16 = unreachable, prevents loops). Two message types: Request, Response. RIPv1 broadcasts and is classful (no mask in updates). RIPv2 multicasts to 224.0.0.9 and is classless (carries mask). Configure: `router rip` → `version 2` → `no auto-summary` → `network <classful-net>`. Per-interface: `passive-interface gi0/0` to suppress sending updates.

cards.front: EIGRP key terms: Feasible Distance, Reported (Advertised) Distance, Successor, Feasible Successor, Feasibility Condition.
cards.back: FD (Feasible Distance) = THIS router's metric to the destination. RD/AD (Reported Distance) = NEIGHBOR's metric to the destination. Successor = best route (lowest FD). Feasible Successor = backup route where neighbor's RD < successor's FD (guarantees loop-free). Feasibility Condition: RD < FD of current successor. EIGRP uses unequal-cost LB ONLY across feasible successors (with `variance N`).

cards.front: EIGRP default K-values and metric formula?
cards.back: K1=1, K2=0, K3=1, K4=0, K5=0. By default the metric uses bandwidth (K1) and delay (K3): metric = (10^7 / min-bandwidth-in-kbps + cumulative-delay/10) × 256. Uses bandwidth of the SLOWEST link plus delay of ALL links. K-values must match between EIGRP neighbors for adjacency.
